reasoning: Since the SOFR 90-day average is a deterministic function of past daily rates, we can mechanically predict its short-term movement. Over the past 90 days (mid-May to mid-August), daily SOFR was mostly in the 4.29-4.33 range, while recent August daily rates are around 4.34-4.36. As these new higher daily rates replace older lower ones in the rolling window, the 90-day average is virtually guaranteed to increase over the next month, making the August 24 and September 16 resolution dates highly probable (both happen before the effects of any late-September rate cut could pull the average down). Conversely, markets are pricing an ~85% chance of a rate cut at the September 17 FOMC meeting, and a 64% chance of two cuts by December. A cut will instantly drop the daily SOFR, swiftly dragging the 90-day average below its August 17 baseline. Thus, the November 15 resolution is very likely resolving NO, assuming cuts materialize. The February 2026 date is even further into expected easing territory, but due to longer-term macroeconomic uncertainty (e.g. inflation rebounding or a policy pivot), the probability strictly pulls back closer to 0.5 per forecasting best practices of increasing uncertainty for distant horizons.
